speaker: interviewee
text: Hi. My name is Barbara Krauss-Schubert, and I'm James's youngest child. I'm 33. And um--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel growing up being a child of a survivor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like my brother said, I'm also very proud. Um, I have a little bit different memory of the stories my father told me, um, more of a positive feeling I got from my father. Not, of course, of what happened, but I guess the stories I heard were more about his liberation and about how the stories of a child stealing bread from outside of the ghetto and actually succeeding at it and how he got back into the ghetto. So I had stories that I think made me feel like my father was like a hero. And he really, you know, was a survivor, but I think, for me, he might have-- being that I was the youngest one-- kept the horrors from me a little bit. Of course, I was raised knowing about it, seeing it on TV and reading. But from his perspective, it was more of how he survived it and how he lived afterwards and how he raised us to achieve success. And you know, that's what I remember. And I also remember being in college. The first time he really sat down with me is I had to do an interview with someone that I respected, and I chose my father. And I interviewed him about the Holocaust then. What I remember most about it was that my-- I did get I think an A minus or a B plus, and my teacher read it out loud to the class and brought basically the whole class to tears.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel it will affect-- having-- being a child of a survivor-- will it affect the way you raise your children? Do you think that you're going to do anything different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I don't know different than my parents did in that respect, but I feel that, definitely, they will know about it. They will know that their grandfather went through this, not only what they learn in school and see on television, but as a personal part of them and their history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel in any way-- both of you or either one of you, whoever wants to answer this question-- that being the child of a survivor has given you any special kind of strengths or weaknesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: One strength I definitely feel I got from it is a total acceptance of other people. I don't understand and I never understood prejudice. I just-- you know, it's ridiculous. You take a look at what we went through, and I mean, that was prejudice to the n-th degree. So any type of prejudice, to me, is just disgusting. It's just-- it's horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that I certainly spend a good portion of my life-- with my children, they're going to-- they're going to osmose that. And I know, I do my little things. If I'm in a business conversation and somebody uses a derogatory term about any race or nationality, I feel compelled just to put my hand up and say, no. You know, I-- just think about that. Please don't use that in my presence. And try to use it as a kind of an opening of the door. Like, why would you even do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I also, uh, you know, have, you know, feeling about anti-Semitism. Obviously, it boggles my mind. But I really do believe that history has shown that it's been here forever, and it's probably going to continue. And you know, I think we all have to keep that in mind because, you know, it's 1997 now. And when I grew up, my idol was Martin Luther King in the '60s. And I just loved what he stood for.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we are in 1997, and you know, if there's ever any type of you know, civil unrest-- it seems like-- for instance, I'm totally confused by why, say, some African-Americans will-- will have animosity towards Jewish people. It astounds me because I feel, as a Jew, I empathize with African-Americans' plights coming over here as slaves. And I say, wait a second. We were there for them in the '60s and the Civil Rights in the South. How can they-- you know, a group of them-- attack Jewish people when Jewish people know what it's like to be, you know, persecuted and we're so empathetic?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I say, that's an example of anti-Semitism not necessarily having any logic. It's just-- it's just there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbara, it there anything that you would like to add or say about your father? Anything at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I love him dearly, and I think he gave me a wonderful childhood. And um, I hope to pass that along to my children. And I think, um, just the fact that he came here with nothing and not knowing the language and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I knew the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me a very happy life. I'm very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. If I can add on that also-- my father, uh, his whole life was just the most dapper, elegant, charming man I ever, ever encountered. And he had a beautiful way of speaking, of telling stories, and just communicating with other people. And he came here, and people first, you know, looked at him as a refugee and heard a strange accent. But oh, he got through that like butter, no problem. Because there was so much inside of him that can just turn people around, and he had business associates of all different nationalities, what have you. And what have you, he just-- you know, whether it was the war, whether it was what he went through, but he made a beautiful life. And I'm very grateful for-- for everything that I have, you know, as a result of him coming through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What more can I say? Just great. Just great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. That was my father and mother. The picture was taken in Budapest, Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I imagine it was taken in 1980-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hold up for one second. Me. Right, chubby me with my mother. I imagine that was in 1931. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That's my brother, Mickey. I imagine this was taken in 19-- uh, 1932 or so, or 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all right. I don't need a acknowledgement that I had to keep the grave up. I don't need it. OK. Next.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you just state what the gravestone-- whose gravestone that is? [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] JK: This is my mother's grave in Budapest. Yes. Who's this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture of me at the age of, um, 21 or 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, uh, New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? Nushe, ?] or Anna. It was taken 30 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is that with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. Her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- this is my granddaughter, Rachel Krauss. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson, David Krauss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, Sarah Krauss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Allison Schubert Krauss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture? Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paul and Barbara, [PAUSES] my daughter and my daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Robert, and my daughter-in-law, Kim.
